sentences.text: Oh, yes, two things that stand out in my mind.
First of all, somewhat dilapidated, to say the least, unshaven and otherwise.
I managed to go up to my parents' flat.
And my father opened the door, and he was quite beside himself to see me.
Remember that three weeks before my actual departure, he wouldn't even speak to me, he was so upset by this.
And he was crying, and he was highly delighted to see me back safely and soundly.
My mother was, of course, equally pleased to see me back, naturally.
[LAUGHS] And for the first time, I had a chance to have a proper wash and have a decent meal, and it was a question of getting back into the normal sort of daily routine.
I then ultimately went back to work again where I'd have to-- you had to use some sort of an excuse.
I think I-- I mentioned that my father, who lived in Birmingham, was taken ill, and I had to look after him, some kind of a sob story.
Because I'd been away there without telling them what I was going to do, that I was going to be away.
I didn't even know for how long.
And that was a little unpleasant and somewhat difficult, but you know, one overcomes these things.
The worst thing they could have done was to kick me out, and in those days, it wouldn't have been too difficult to find another job.

I then started training the people drawn from our movement, from Habonim, and we used the facilities that existed then and I think still exist today at the headquarters of Maccabi in London at Compayne Gardens.
And I remember we were doing some unarmed combat when suddenly there was quite some noise, and the doors were being pushed open, broken open.
And there were some officials, I think in civilian clothes-- yes.
It was obvious that they were Scotland Yard or some sort of police.
And I made-- I made a very quick escape through the window, but the window was on ground level.
I didn't dive, you know, from the first or second or fourth floor, and I managed to get the away.
And I think they were fundamentally interested in not arresting anybody but just making sure that these sort of things would not go on that were obviously against the interests of the government.
Yes.
This must been early in 1948, possibly in February or March.
Exact dates I really don't know.
Oh, yes.
I mean, that happened throughout the-- the whole time.
You see, the Sochnut, the leadership of the Jewish community in Palestine, known as the Sochnut, consisted of people like Ben-Gurion.
Moshe Shertok, as he was named.
Later on, he changed his name to Sharett, became foreign minister.
Golda Meir, Berl Katznelson, very, very prominent people.
And in all their negotiations, they had to come to London because that is where the government, which was, after all, in charge of the mandate in Palestine, was situated.

And every time these people were here in London, the word used to come around through all the youth leaders, of whom I was one-- and there were about 16 or 17 of us in London-- and the old man is at the [?
Bayit, ?] and the [?
Bayit ?] was, in fact, the headquarters, or was a hostel, a Habonim hostel, somewhere in East London, in Clapton, I think.
I don't remember its exact surrounding.
And so we would, in fact, spend the evening and ultimately the night there.
We would never sleep because these people would talk to us, imbue us with their plans, with their enthusiasm.
They were personalities of an-- of an outstanding sort of-- outstanding personalities, and they imbued us with a lot of enthusiasm.
And then at weekends, we had our own group meetings, again, and so that's where we imparted that enthusiasm to our numbers.
It was a fantastic experience.
Yes, yes.
Yes, yes.
No.
Because it was always in groups, and they were psychologically very, very capable, I think.
They never singled out a specific individual.

In other words, an individual never established a very-- to my knowledge, at least, and certainly I never developed, or given the opportunity to develop, a very personal relationship with any one of them.
Possibly for security, secondly because their concept was, after all, have a youth team operation, teamwork, and so on.
And on that basis, no, I don't have any-- any very specific sort of the reminiscences of any-- any-- any-- any particular relationship.
But certainly, they knew my face.
Certainly, I knew them, because the whole world knew them.
But just to be in their company and to listen to what, in fact, their plans were.
What they-- obviously, they were not plans that were considered to be secret in any way.
They wouldn't divulge that.
But just to talk about the whole concept, the liberation, the ideas that one had and what one would do with a country or one's own, and so on, was, you know, incredibly inspiring.
Well, the full hog, in so far that I then went on a kibbutz Hachshara.
That was a training farm, one of six or seven in the country, and this was situated in Bosham in Hampshire between Chichester and Portsmouth, or as the locals call it, Pompey and Chi.
And there we had a building.
It had been a-- a I don't quite know how to call this.
A [INAUDIBLE].
It used to be a recuperation home, a rest home, a rest home for employees of Gestetner.
Gestetner was a large company.
Sigmund Gestetner himself was a very ardent Zionist.
He had established another rest home for his employees, and he'd made this over to the Zionist organization.
And there were about 40 of us there, boys and girls, and we were fundamentally all city dwellers, city people.
And so it was a question of getting used to two major items.
First of all, hard, physical work, and that was not too difficult.
And the other aspect, obviously, was to live in a community, to have basically nothing or everything.
In other words, you had no private property, and everything belonged to the commune.

So on Friday nights, for instance, to give you an example, we would always have a Shabbat meal, and we would always have a sing song, and we would always put up a show of some sort of entertainment, sometimes of a very high cultural standard.
Many of these people were highly cultured individuals from universities, academically well-qualified, technically well-qualified, militarily well-qualified.
It was after the Second World War.
We had a number of people of the rank of officers, and so on, in the British forces there.
Age ranged between 18 and perhaps 26 or 27, and boys and girls were living there.
And on Fridays, when we came back from work.
Of course, we'd have showers, and we'd shave.
The boys would shave, and so on, and then on our beds we would find a white shirt and a clean pair of trousers.
And that shirt may have had somebody else's' name on them.
I mean, it was never your own shirt.
It was one that would fit you because the girls who were working in the laundry would obviously know your details, you know?
What collar size you were.
But since they were all open-neck shirts, in any case, it didn't matter if you had a size or two too large.
That didn't matter as long as it wasn't too small, because you have to button it up in the front.
So everything was commonly owned, and all the duties that were performed there were decided by the Vaad.
That means the commission.
Commission.
People were elected to those sort of things in exactly the same way as they were in the proper kibbutz.
The only thing we didn't have on these training farms were we didn't have little children because people were single.
Although some, in fact, married, and there were one or two couples who joined us who were already married, but we had no children.
So other than that, we had this magnificent training farm of 350 acres or so, and after some time, I became the foreman of the farm gang.
Most of our people worked in local farms as laborers, and others worked as laborers, like I did, you know, even the farm foreman on the actual farm.
And this was very convivial, wonderful.
The thing that impressed me very much was the incredibly high moral standard, because there were verile men, and very young and very sort of beautiful girls there and so on.
We never had any hanky panky business.
Just we had couples that were obviously very much going together, and they would, you know, go out for a walk in the evening or something of that sort.
But you know, in the house, in the building itself, it was 100% clean.
We never had any problems whatsoever.
I wonder what under present circumstances such a close knit community would actually achieve or otherwise fail to achieve under the present circumstances.
OK.
And after that, since I was already registered somehow with the Haganah.
They showed interest in my joining the armed forces in Israel, and so I went to Israel after exactly one year's full rotation on the farm, which was a wonderful experience.
I loved every minute of it.

And I went to the army, and the army in Israel had a specific unit which was known as Cheil Mada, the Scientific Corps, which consisted fundamentally of engineers, physicists, chemists, mathematicians who were engaged in development of weaponry.
And my function, I was not a qualified engineer at that stage, but I'd had practical experience as an apprentice tool maker.
And this came in useful in so far that after you designed and modified certain things, they after all had to be made as well.
And so we had to form a-- a-- a-- a-- a-- a-- a-- workshop, a factory.
This was between Haifa and Akko in Haifa Bay.
It was not too far away from a village that was called Kiryat Ata, where Ata was a major textile manufacturer, and very close to a kibbutz that was named after the Czech president Masaryk.
It was called Kfar Masaryk.
And--
I'm 71, nearly 71.
Oh, then.
Well, let's go back.
Born '26.
By that time, I must have been about 25, 26, something of that nature, yes.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there-- we, the building-- building this factory was fascinating because we got all the machinery as presents from various organizations throughout the world.
And the biggest headache was that there were all these machines, some of them quite complex for those days, nothing like machine tools are today with computer control and so on.
But nevertheless, complex for those days and had to be assembled.
And very often, certain parts were missing where you had to sort of figure out how does that get together with that.
They were fundamentally all surplus war equipment, mainly from the United States, from the United Kingdom, from Italy, and from France.
And one of my tasks, particularly, was to try and assemble these machines, not entirely on my own.
We had-- we had a complement of about 180 craftsmen there from all over the world, incidentally.
All Jewish, but not necessarily always a common language.
But in engineering, this is not too difficult, because you can always sketch things, your ideas and so on and so forth.
So that really presented no problem.
Language was a problem when you were involved in some combat, and the combat unit.
Yes.
Yes, I was trying for that in case there were any other conflagrations, short periods of war.
[INAUDIBLE].
But I was never involved in any major campaign, such as, for instance, the Six Day War or-- or-- or perhaps the, you know, the-- the-- the Battle of the Suez Canal, and so on.
By that time, I was long out of the country again.
I came back to England again.
No, I didn't settle in Israel for a very, very good reason.
I realized, particularly having been in that unit, that it was very important to get a-- a basic degree in engineering.
And while I was offered a place at the Technion, which is the Technical University in Haifa, I felt rather worried about this, and I'll tell you quite honestly why.
I was never a terribly good student.

And the combination of the material, which was tough, with a foreign language, which I spoke but had great difficulties in writing or reading-- because I learned the language via the ears, by hearing it, by speaking it, and so on-- I thought it would be easier if I could do that back in England.
And so I wrote to the Dean of the faculty of the college at which I studied early on.
And--
Well, it was called The Northampton College of Advanced Technology in those days.
Today it's called the City University.
And it's part of London University.
Today, it's a university in its own right, but in those days it was attached to the University of London, yes.
And I would go back there as a part time student purely and simply because I couldn't afford to maintain myself without having any income.
And from an engineering point of view, that was good, because while you learned the theoretical part in the evenings, you actually executed the practical aspects during the day.
This was the finest combination you could possibly have.
It was tough.
It lasted six, seven years, but it was worth it in the end.
And the Dean was very appreciative.
He remembered me well because I was quite an outstanding bloke before I left.
I scored one mark in the mathematics paper, and so I went down in the annals of the college history.
And incidentally, I've recently just been invited to join the alumni group, so that's rather nice.
But--
Yes.
[INAUDIBLE] I had to start all over again, yes.
But this is why the Dean remembered me.
[LAUGHS] He said we'd be delighted to have you back, but you've got to start at the rock bottom again, which I was prepared to do.
Yes.
I-- I took qualification in mechanical engineering.

But always having been very interested in human beings and-- and-- and-- and human contacts, I then took an additional qualification in production or industrial engineering, as it's known in America, or process engineering, which gives you far more contact with human beings.

And ultimately, through various jobs that I selected myself-- I stayed in a job no more than two years because in those days, jobs were easy to be obtained, especially in engineering, it gave me a chance always to advance rather than wait until, in fact, the vacancy occurred.
And ultimately, I became chief production engineer in an organization, and then was asked-- no, then I applied for a job with an American company of management consultants.
And I got a job with them, and I worked for a short time in Sweden, and in the United States, and Canada, and then I was asked to go to Germany.
Oh, yes.
I returned to England.
I returned to England, and I completed my studies here in England.
Oh, the homeland.
Yes, well, I don't look upon it like that, but I did return to Germany, yes, yes, yes.
I consider it to be a foreign country [INAUDIBLE] as any others.
Well, I discussed that to some extent with my wife, and that's really-- I was married then, and we had--
We had our first child on the way.

But I had very mixed feelings, but the thing that attracted me was that as a management consultant, I needed to get a wider aspect of practical management, not only just in the United Kingdom, and Germany had been extremely successful to build itself up again after the War to a large extent through its management and its approach to-- to-- to complex situations.
And so I felt I could learn a lot over there which would stand me in good stead later on when I came back to the United Kingdom.
And ostensibly, I was supposed to go there for a year, but this extended to two years, to three years, and 3 and 1/2 years.
And then finally, I got back here by sheer luck.
A British, large engineering company, a steel plant in Sheffield, was very interested to see what this American organization was doing in Germany.
And they were quite impressed, and they said we'd like to do the same thing here in the UK, but could we do it with Steve?
Because they were over there for three days, and they saw what I was doing there.
I acted as an interpreter as well.
And so we came to Sheffield, and this is where we still are today, you see.
Thank you.
But before you came to Sheffield, how did you actually feel about being back in Germany, the country from which you'd fled?
It was difficult at first, but ultimately, I found that there were a lot of people-- there were of course, this happened in the late-- early '60s, 1960, '65, round about that time.

And the Germans were still very, very conscious of the War, A, that they'd lost it, and B, of the atrocities that they had committed not only towards Jews, but to many other what they called 'minority' or 'minor' sort of societies-- Poles, Ukrainians, Russians, Gypsies, Estonians, and so on and so forth.

And it was at first a little difficult, especially since I had to be very careful not to let them understand or let them to get to know that I was, in fact, born in Germany and that I was a refugee, and that would have been harmful to my basically business associations with them.
I was there on a business term.
I was hired into the company to train the system in some technological development, whatever it may have been.
And if they would have known I was born in Germany and Jewish, I would have had to suffer from a lot of emotional problems.
They would have had either a lot of sympathy with me, or otherwise they would have hated my guts.
Neither of those things would have been helpful in a purely business association.
But after a few years in Germany and reflecting upon it, I found a lot of very good people there.
By good, I'm mean human beings-- not good as engineers, but as human beings-- that they fundamentally have no other interests than we have.
And the fact that many of them got involved in this sort of Nazi ideology and were literally forced to play a part in it was their misfortune.
Now, we certainly can criticize them.
I'd often discussed that them with them.
Why did you make it so easy to join in their sort of throng?
They basically had no alternatives.
There were, of course, some real bastards amongst them, obviously.
There's no doubt about it.

But I think the majority of people, particularly today, while they still feel very, very affected by the sins of their fathers, for which one can hardly blame them, today's generation, at least, they are basically reasonable individuals.
You said that you felt England as your homeland.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes, that was quite an experience.

When I came back from Israel, I applied for British nationality, and having rejected it the first time when my parents were offered British nationality and granted it, because I found myself at variance with the then government's policy on Palestine.
They were offered, and I was interviewed in 1947, I think.
Again, terrible upheaval between my father and myself, you know.
He thought this was dreadful.
But nevertheless, I made my own decision quite clearly, and I said, no, I'm at variance with the policy on Palestine, and in no way would I-- fair enough.
So when I came back, I applied.
I'd been out of the country, sum total, for just five years and one or two weeks, and therefore, I had to start serving all again.
And I didn't apply again for British nationality until, I think, '58 or '59.
You had to chalk up another five years of residence.
Yes, exactly that.
And then I went for an interview.
I lived in London.

I went for an interview at the home office, and the normal sort of stoic and very stiff officialdom that exists between this exalted official and the nebbish of an individual who wants to become British.
And this guy presented-- had a large book with him.
And he opened this up, and he read me exact dates when I went over to the continent, what I did, where I was based.
I mean, not on the front here, but where the headquarters were of the illegal immigration somewhere in Paris, and so on and so forth.
He had everything there, exact dates and everything else.
And my heart was sinking lower and lower because, you know, I felt I'd wasted another 20 pounds--
Yeah, that's right.
But in the end, he took out his big stamp, and he banged this onto my application form.
And he stood up, and he shook hands with me, and he said, fortunately, of course, our two countries are now at peace.
You've got to remember that I put down on my present nationality, on my application form, of course, Israeli, of which I was immensely proud.
I didn't have to put German anymore or stateless.
I put down Israeli.
And so, he said, fortunately our two countries are at peace, and this is a good thing.
And he also said-- I remember that-- he says I envy you for your experiences.
I doubt if I would have had the same drive or guts or whatever, he sort of said, had I been given that sort of opportunity.
And so here I was, you see, of dual nationality.
I would just like to say this, that it is difficult to imagine any other country that could have been perhaps quite so-- so-- so-- so open minded in a situation of this nature.
After all, I never killed anybody.
I never took up arms against Britain.

But what I did there was obviously against-- or what I participated in-- was obviously against their national interest, and so it would have been very easy for them to say, well, sorry, Joe, but you know, you've shown us your colors once.
We don't particularly want to accept you.
And I think that's the greatness in the British system.
However much we sort of criticize it at times-- there's a lot, unfortunately, nowadays to be criticized-- there comes out the greatness of this nation, I think.
And I would like this to be-- that's why I mentioned it-- I would like that to be recorded, I think, out of fairness, yes.
Oh, I see.
Oh, another tape.
